coredata.dc:title: Blue and green photoluminescence graphene quantum dots synthesized from carbon fibers

Su, Yanjie
Lu, Xiaonan
Zhang, Yaozhong
Yang, Zhi
Zhang, Yafei
coredata.dc:description: A facile chemical oxidation and exfoliation method has been developed to prepare luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using commercial carbon fibers as the raw material, which were synthesized from pitch through an annealing process at 1500°C. The as-prepared GQDs, in the thickness range of 1–2 graphene layers, exhibit an excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) behavior similar to that of semiconductor quantum dots, and can be dissolved in water and common polar solvents. This approach has enabled a simple and effective way for production of the GQDs solution, which could be separated into blue and green photoluminescence (PL) GQDs with different dialysis bags.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Carbon materials
Luminescence
Oxidation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image and (b) XRD pattern of the pitch-based CF.

(a) Raman spectra of the GQDs and CF and (b) FTIR spectrum of the GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) AFM image of the GQDs deposited on freshly cleaved mica substrates; (b) Height profile along the line in (a); and (c) Height distribution of the GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs, inset: photograph taken under visible light; (b) PL (at 373nm excitation) spectra of the GQDs, inset: photograph taken under 365nm UV light; (c) PL spectra of GQDs at 300–460nm excitation wavelengths; and (d) PL (at 294nm excitation) spectra of the GQDs outside, inside the dialysis bag and the mixture, inset: photograph of them taken under 365nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Blue and green photoluminescence graphene quantum dots synthesized from carbon fibers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology, Ministry of Education, Research Institute of Micro/Nanometer Science and Technology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile chemical oxidation and exfoliation method has been developed to prepare luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using commercial carbon fibers as the raw material, which were synthesized from pitch through an annealing process at 1500°C. The as-prepared GQDs, in the thickness range of 1–2 graphene layers, exhibit an excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) behavior similar to that of semiconductor quantum dots, and can be dissolved in water and common polar solvents. This approach has enabled a simple and effective way for production of the GQDs solution, which could be separated into blue and green photoluminescence (PL) GQDs with different dialysis bags.

Water and organic-soluble graphene quantum dots with thickness about 1-2 graphene layers were fabricated by chemical oxidation method and their photoluminescence (PL) properties were excitation-independent. The solution could be further separated into blue and green PL GQDs with different dialysis bags.

► Single or double-layer graphene quantum dots were successfully synthesized via a simple and effective route. ► Presence of O-rich groups makes the GQDs soluble in aqueous medium and common organic solvents. ► As-prepared GQDs displayed excitation-independent PL behavior. ► Products could be separated into blue and green photoluminescent GQDs by different dialysis bags.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, as a new type of quantum dots, graphene quantum dot (GQD) has attracted more and more attention due to its chemical inertness, biocompatibility and low toxicity [1,2]. The GQDs have the excellent performance of graphene such as high carrier transport mobility, superior mechanical flexibility and environmentally friendly nature [3,4]. Moreover, converting the 2D graphene sheets into the 0D GQDs can enlarge the electronic and opto-electronic application due to strong quantum confinement and edge effects [5,6].

Most works on GQDs have been focused on theoretical prediction and the experimental synthesis is only a recent effort. Recently, Pan et al. [7,8] developed a hydrothermal method to synthesize blue or green luminescent GQDs through cutting graphene sheets. Li et al. [9] reported an electrochemical means of synthesizing green-luminescent functional GQDs. Lu et al. [10] presented a mechanistic approach to the synthesis of atomically defined GQDs by metal-catalyzed cage-opening of C60. However, these methods are limited by the availability of special equipment, extremely expensive materials and long production phase.

In this letter, we develop a simple and effective approach to easily synthesize photoluminescent GQDs using commercial carbon fibers (CFs) as the raw material. This method consists of chemical oxidation and exfoliation route, which is always used to solubilize the carbon nanoparticles [11,12]. The as-synthesized GQDs can freely disperse in water and organic solvents without further chemical modification and ultrasonic dispersion. Interestingly, the as-synthesized GQDs possess excitation-independent PL properties and can be separated into blue and green photoluminescent GQDs by different dialysis bags.

0.6g CFs were dispersed in concentrated H2SO4 (120ml) and HNO3 (40ml) for 5h under continuous ultrasonic treatments (100W), and then the mixed solution was refluxed for 24h at 100°C with magnetic stirring. After the above-mentioned reaction, the solution was diluted with double distilled water (1500ml), and the pH was adjusted to 8 with Na2CO3. The resulting suspension was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 2000Da) for 3 days.

The morphologies of the samples were observed by SEM (Carl Zeiss Ultra55) and AFM (SII Nanonavi E-Sweep). XRD analyses were carried on D/max 2550VL/PC instrument with Cu Kα radiation. The PL spectra were performed using a spectrophotometer (HITACHI, F-4600) with slit width of 0.5mm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer. Raman spectroscopy was characterized using Bruker Senterra dispersive Raman microscopy with laser wavelengths at 532nm. FTIR spectrum was recorded on a Bruker (Germany) VERTEC 80v vacuum FTIR spectrometer.

Pitch-based CF is a kind of microcrystal graphite material, which is processed by carbonization and graphitization using organic fibers. From Fig. 1a, one can see that the CF has a large crystallite dimension and high degree of orientation. The XRD pattern in Fig. 1b shows a broad (002) peak centered at around 30.4° and the interlayer spacing is calculated to be 0.36nm, which is broader than that of graphite [13]. It can be attributed to the thinness and disordered stacking of the CF. Fig. 2a shows the Raman spectrum (black line); it is obvious that the CF has the small domain structure of sp2 carbons that makes it easy to extract GQDs, since the bandgap and the PL spectrum partly depend on the fraction of the sp2 domains [14,15].

Raman spectrum (red line) of the GQDs is shown in Fig. 2a, which has been usually used to confirm the quality of the prepared GQDs. One can see two major Raman peaks at 1374cm−1 (D band) and 1580.5cm−1 (G band). The intensity ratio ID/IG is 0.89, which is similar with the high quality few-layer graphene nanoribbons [16,17]. The G peak shows redshift compared with the original CF, which is opposite to the GQDs prepared by another CF [18]. The unique small ratio indicates fewer defects of the GQDs developed by chemical oxidation and exfoliation method than those synthesized by other methods [7,9].

We further characterize the as-synthesized GQDs with FTIR technology; as shown in Fig. 2b, the result reveals a predominant functionalization of the GQDs with hydroxyl –OH group (ʋOH≈3448cm−1) and carboxyl group (–COOH, ʋCOOH≈1624cm−1). The presence of O-rich groups makes the GQDs soluble in aqueous medium and provides additional advantages for further foundation to meet various requirements. Apart from the solubility of the GQDs in aqueous medium, the as-synthesized GQDs can also be well dispersed in common organic solvents such as chlorobenzene and DMF, which facilitate the applications in non-aqueous phase and organic device.

The morphologies of the as-synthesized GQDs were observed by AFM, as shown in Fig. 3c. It can be seen clearly that the GQDs have a narrow height distribution from 0.3 to 0.9nm, suggesting that the GQDs typically consist of 1–2 graphene layers. The majority of heights detected are among 0.3–0.4nm, which is similar with the interlayer spacing of bulk graphite (0.334nm), indicating that most of the GQDs are actually separate graphenes [19].

Fig. 4 shows UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra of the as-synthesized GQDs. From the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a), a typical absorption band at 373nm can be observed, which is assigned to the π→π⁎ transition of graphitic sp2 domains [20]. The PL spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows a strong peak at 507nm when excited at 373nm, and the full width at half maximum (FWHM) is about 100nm, which approximates that of the most reported carbon nanodots [7–12]. Insets of Fig. 4a and b are photographs of the water-dispersed GQDs illuminated under sunlight and 365nm UV light; the green fluorescence color can be observed by naked eye.

To further characterize the optical properties of the as-synthesized GQDs, a detailed PL investigation was carried out with different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4c). When the excited wavelengths change from 300 to 460nm, the PL intensity decrease remarkably, but the fluorescence emission peak remained unshifted. This excitation-independent PL behavior is different from most of the reported carbon-based nanomaterials [7–10], which were dependent on excitation wavelength and always shifted to longer wavelengths. This special feature may result from less surface defects and more uniform size of GQDs [21].

The most distinctive feature of the GQDs which sets them apart from other previously reported carbon dots is, inter alia, the specific process of separating green and blue luminescence GQDs. The dialysis process allows the separation of GQDs from larger molecules through a semi-permeable membrane with 2000Da MWCO. Interestingly, the dialysis process not only purifies the GQDs but also makes different optical properties from inside to outside of the dialysis bag (Fig. 4d). The outside solution is further dialyzed using the dialysis bag of 1000Da MWCO. When excited at 294nm, the PL spectrum of the GQDs outside the dialysis bag shows that two peaks at 340nm and 450–500nm can be seen and the peak at 340nm is stronger, which is opposite to the GQDs inside of the dialysis bag. The spectrum of the mixture solution presents a comprehensive feature. For comparison, most of the reported blue-luminescence GQDs had a longer wavelength at the absorption peak [7,14]. The photographs of the inside, the outside and the mixture GQDs taken under 365nm UV light are shown in the inset of Fig. 4d. This selective separation process confirms that the different sizes GQDs show quantum size effect, which can contribute to PL properties.

In summary, we have demonstrated a unique strategy to simultaneously synthesize GQDs in large scale with chemical oxidation and exfoliation route and to further separate different sizes by dialyzing in different dialysis bags. This facile method shows prominent advantages of low-cost and mass-manufacturability over the reported ones. The as-synthesized GQDs show broad UV–vis absorption and excitation-independent PL behavior. Furthermore, the highly crystalline and ultrasmall GQDs would provide a novel nanoplatform for manipulating graphene-based electronics in a solution state.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial support from National High-Tech R&D Program of China (No. 2011AA050504), Shanghai Science and Technology( Grant no. 12nm0503800) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61006002). We also thank Instrumental Analysis Center of SJTU for the support in AFM measurements.
